2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173 introduced
100	    the \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} class
101	    (\ref{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}) bundling this conjunction;
102	    the order/principal lemmas downstream take this class as a typeclass
103	    hypothesis when they require the DVR structure at codim-1 points.
104	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
105	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
106	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
107	    \begin{quote}
108	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
109	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
111	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
112	    \end{quote}
113	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
114	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
115	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
116	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
117	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
118	    properness; integrality threaded).
119	\end{itemize}
120	\noindent
121	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
122	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
123	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
124	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
125	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
126	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
127	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
128	\begin{definition}
129	\leanok
130	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
131	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
133	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
134	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
135	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
137	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
138	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
139	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
140	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
141	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
142	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
143	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
144	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
146	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
147	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
148	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
149	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
150	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
152	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
153	  the Mathlib predicate
154	  \[
155	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
156	  \]
157	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
158	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
159	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
160	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
161	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
162	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
163	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
164	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
165	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
166	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
167	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
168	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
169	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
170	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
171	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
172	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
173	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
174	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
175	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
176	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
177	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
178	  \begin{itemize}
179	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
180	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
181	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
182	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
183	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
184	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
185	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
186	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
187	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
188	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
189	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
190	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
191	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
192	      uses.
193	  \end{itemize}
194	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
195	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
196	  needs.
197	\end{definition}
198	\begin{definition}
199	\leanok
200	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
201	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
202	  
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
204	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
205	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
206	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
207	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
209	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
210	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
211	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
212	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
214	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
215	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
216	  \[
217	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
218	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
219	  \]
220	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
221	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
222	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
223	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
226	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
227	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
228	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
229	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
230	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
231	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
232	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
233	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
234	\end{definition}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Regular in codimension one]
238	  \label{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne}
240	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II \S6, p.~130, the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)
241	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
242	  % Stacks pointers: tag 0BX5 (regular schemes), tag 02ME
243	  % (DVR-of-stalk characterisation at codim-1 points).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme
245	  % which is regular in codimension one."
246	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130 (the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)).}
247	  A scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in codimension one} when for every point
248	  \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) (equivalently,
249	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1\)) the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is
250	  a discrete valuation ring. The Lean encoding bundles this conjunction as
251	  a one-field \texttt{Prop} class so that downstream order / principal /
252	  degree lemmas may consume it as a typeclass hypothesis.
253	\end{definition}
254	\section{Open-immersion descent for prime divisors}
255	\label{sec:open_immersion_descent}
256	\textit{Source: Stacks Project tags 02IZ (stalk under open immersion) +
257	01J6 (open immersion preserves regularity); cross-referenced with the
258	project-local iter-183 substrate
259	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}.}
260	
261	The chapter's order / principal-divisor API is naturally stated on the
262	ambient scheme \(X\), but several downstream proofs (the
263	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) non-zero branch in
264	particular) require localising to an affine open \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
265	identifying prime divisors of \(X\) lying in \(U\) with prime divisors of
266	\(U\), and transporting stalk-level data along the open-immersion stalk
267	isomorphism. This section records the substrate bijection
268	\(\{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
269	 \;\simeq\; U.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\)
270	and the open-immersion descent of the \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
271	class.
272	
273	\begin{definition}
274	[Restriction of a prime divisor to an open subscheme]
275	  \label{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen}
277	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
278	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), a prime
279	  divisor \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), and a witness \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\),
280	  the restriction \(Y |_U : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages
281	  the point \(Y.\texttt{point}\) (viewed as a point of \(U\) via the open
282	  inclusion) with the coheight witness from
283	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) (project-local
284	  substrate, iter-183 \texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185).
285	\end{definition}
286	\begin{definition}
287	[Push of a prime divisor along an open immersion]
288	  \label{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
289	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen}
290	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
291	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), and a
292	  prime divisor \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), the push
293	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages the point
294	  \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U \hookrightarrow X\) (viewed as a point of \(X\))
295	  with the coheight witness from
296	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) applied in the
297	  opposite direction.
298	\end{definition}
299	\begin{lemma}
300	[Bijection of prime divisors under open immersion]
301	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_equivOpen}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen}
303	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen, def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
304	  For an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\) of a scheme \(X\), there is a
305	  bijection
306	  \[
307	    \{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
308	    \;\simeq\;
309	    U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}
310	  \]
311	  packaging the restriction (\ref{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}) and the
312	  push (\ref{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}) as mutual inverses. Both
313	  \texttt{left\_inv} and \texttt{right\_inv} hold definitionally
314	  (\texttt{rfl}) because both directions preserve the underlying point
315	  field via subtype eta + structure eta.
316	\end{lemma}
317	\begin{lemma}
318	[Stalk isomorphism across an open immersion]
319	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}
320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso}
321	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
322	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02IZ (stalk is invariant under restriction
323	  % to a non-empty open neighbourhood) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex).
324	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, tag 02IZ, plus Mathlib's
325	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}.}
326	  For \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\), the
327	  stalk presheaf of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) at the restricted point coincides,
328	  up to canonical isomorphism, with the stalk presheaf of \(X\) at
329	  \(Y.\texttt{point}\). The Lean wrapper packages Mathlib's
330	  \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\) for the project's
331	  \texttt{PrimeDivisor} bundle.
332	\end{lemma}
333	\begin{theorem}
334	[\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) descends along open immersion]
335	  \label{thm:isRegularInCodimensionOne_open}
336	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen}
337	  \uses{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne, lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso,
338	    def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
339	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
340	  and \(U \hookrightarrow X\) an open subscheme with
341	  \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}\) (the latter is \emph{not}
342	  automatic from integrality of \(X\); it is threaded explicitly).
343	  Then \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) also satisfies
344	  \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\); the DVR-of-stalk property at a
345	  codim-1 point \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) is
346	  transported from the corresponding point
347	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) on the ambient
348	  scheme via the stalk isomorphism (\ref{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso})
349	  together with
350	  \(\texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing}\)
351	  on the ring-equiv image.
352	\end{theorem}
353	\paragraph{Lean-side note.} The 8 axiom-clean iter-200 substrate
354	declarations
355	(\(\texttt{PrimeDivisor.ext}\), \(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\),
356	\(\texttt{ofOpen}\), the two \(\texttt{@[simp]}\) field-extraction
357	helpers \(\texttt{restrictToOpen\_point}\) /
358	\(\texttt{ofOpen\_point}\), \(\texttt{equivOpen}\),
359	\(\texttt{stalkIso}\), and the descent instance
360	\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen}\)) consume the project-local
361	substrate \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\)
362	(\texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185) and Mathlib's
363	\(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\). Realised cost: \(+120\) LOC over
364	\texttt{WeilDivisor.lean} L153--L305 (iter-200).
365	
366	\paragraph{End-to-end map: Sub-builds 1--3 \(\Rightarrow\) closure of
367	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) (\ref{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}, non-zero branch L535).}
368	The L535 closure target is: for an integral, locally-Noetherian scheme
369	\(X\) satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) and a non-zero
370	\(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
371	\((\texttt{Function.support}\,(Y \mapsto \texttt{order}\,Y\,f)).\texttt{Finite}\).
372	Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV proves this by reducing to an
373	affine open neighbourhood and citing height-1 prime decomposition
374	on a Noetherian ring. The project decomposes the proof into three
375	project-local Sub-builds plus a terminal closure step:
376	
377	\begin{itemize}
378	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 1 (CLOSED iter-200, 8 axiom-clean decls
379	    L153--L305).} Open-immersion descent of the
380	    \(\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) bundle: bijection between
381	    prime divisors of \(X\) lying in an open \(U\) and prime divisors
382	    of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\); stalk-isomorphism wrapper around
383	    Mathlib's \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\); descent of the
384	    \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) class.
385	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 2 (iter-201 target, \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC).}
386	    Naturality of \(\texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\) across a ring isomorphism
387	    \(R \simeq S\) of Noetherian-domain stalks of Krull dim
388	    \(\leq 1\), with compatible fraction-field iso \(K_R \simeq K_S\).
389	    Build via Mathlib's \(\texttt{IsFractionRing.ringEquivOfRingEquiv}\)
390	    + naturality of \(\texttt{Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom}\) at the
391	    base ring.
392	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 3 (iter-202 target, \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC).}
393	    Naturality of \(\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}\) across the
394	    stalk iso of Sub-build 1: for
395	    \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\)
396	    and \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
397	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f
398	     = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,U\,Y\,h)\,f'\)
399	    where \(f'\) is the image of \(f\) under
400	    \(X.\texttt{functionField} \simeq U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{functionField}\)
401	    (Mathlib provides this iso for integral \(X\) via the irreducibility
402	    of the generic point lying in any non-empty open). Direct consumer
403	    of Sub-build 2.
404	  \item \textbf{Terminal closure (iter-203 target, \(\sim 40\)--\(80\) LOC).}
405	    Given a non-zero \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\):
406	    (a) reduce to a finite affine cover \(X = \bigcup_{i = 1}^N U_i\)
407	    (Hartshorne II.6 hypothesis; project-side
408	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]} or just
409	    \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]}); (b) by Sub-build 3, every
410	    \(Y \in \texttt{support}\) lies in some \(U_i\) and has
411	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,
412	     U_i\,Y\,h)\,f_i\) for \(f_i\) the corresponding fraction-field
413	    element on \(U_i\); (c) each \(U_i\) is affine Noetherian, so the
414	    height-1 prime decomposition of \(\mathrm{div}(f_i)\) in
415	    \(\Gamma(U_i, \mathcal O)\) is finite by Mathlib's
416	    \(\texttt{Ideal.finite\_minimalPrimes\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\)
417	    applied to the principal ideal \((f_i)\); (d) bound
418	    \(|\texttt{support}|\) by the sum of the finitely many
419	    \(|\texttt{support of div}(f_i)|\) terms; (e) conclude finiteness.
420	\end{itemize}
421	
422	The chain terminates: each Sub-build is bounded in scope to a
423	specific project-local naturality / descent step; Sub-build 3 is a
424	direct consumer of Sub-build 2 with no further branching; the
425	terminal closure is bounded by the cardinality of a finite affine
426	cover. The structural blocker around the
427	\(\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}\) vs.\
428	\(\texttt{[IsNoetherian X]}\) hypothesis gap (consumer-propagation
429	USER-blocked, see \texttt{STRATEGY.md} A.4.a row) does \emph{not}
430	affect Sub-build 2 or Sub-build 3, which are stated on the open
431	chart \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) and consume only local hypotheses; the
432	terminal closure step is where the hypothesis-strength choice
433	becomes load-bearing.
434	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
435	\begin{definition}
436	\leanok
437	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
438	  \label{def:order_at_point}
439	  
440	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
442	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
443	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
444	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
445	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
447	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
448	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
449	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
450	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
451	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
452	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
453	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
454	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
455	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
456	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
457	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
458	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
459	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
460	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
461	  \[
462	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
463	  \]
464	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
465	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
466	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
467	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
468	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
469	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
470	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
471	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
472	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
473	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
474	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
475	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
476	  \begin{quote}
477	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
478	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
479	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
480	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
481	  \end{quote}
482	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
483	  see below). The body is
484	  \[
485	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
486	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
487	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
488	      \bigr).
489	  \]
490	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
491	  \begin{itemize}
492	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
493	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
494	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
495	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
496	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
497	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
498	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
499	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
500	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
501	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
502	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
503	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
504	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
505	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
506	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
507	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
508	      X.functionField} via
509	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
510	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
511	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
512	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
513	      and from the explicitly threaded
514	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
515	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
516	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
517	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
518	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
519	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
520	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
521	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
522	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
523	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
524	  \end{itemize}
525	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
526	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
527	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
528	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
529	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
530	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
531	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
532	  signature.
533	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
534	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
535	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
536	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
537	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
538	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
539	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
540	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
541	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
542	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
543	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
544	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
545	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
546	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
547	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
548	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
549	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
550	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
551	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
552	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
553	\end{definition}
554	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
555	\begin{definition}
556	\leanok
557	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
558	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
559	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
560	  
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
562	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
563	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
564	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
565	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
566	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
567	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
568	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
569	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
570	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
571	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
572	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
573	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
574	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
575	  \[
576	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
577	  \]
578	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
579	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
580	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
581	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
582	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
583	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
584	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
585	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
586	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
587	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
588	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
589	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
590	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
591	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
592	  codim-\(1\) regime:
593	  \[
594	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
595	      \begin{cases}
596	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
597	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
598	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
599	      \end{cases}
600	  \]
601	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
602	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
603	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
604	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
605	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
606	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
607	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
608	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
609	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
610	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
611	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
612	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
613	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
614	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
615	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
616	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
617	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
618	\end{definition}
619	\begin{lemma}
620	\leanok
621	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
622	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
624	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
625	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
626	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
627	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
628	  then
629	  \[
630	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
631	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
632	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
633	  \]
634	\end{lemma}
635	\begin{proof}
636	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
637	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
638	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
639	\end{proof}
640	\begin{lemma}
641	\leanok
642	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
643	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
644	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
645	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
646	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
647	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
648	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
649	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
650	  scheme), then
651	  \[
652	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
653	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
654	  \]
655	\end{lemma}
656	\begin{proof}
657	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
658	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
659	  negative branch using the hypothesis
660	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
661	\end{proof}
662	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
663	\begin{definition}
664	\leanok
665	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
666	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
667	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
668	  
669	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
670	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
671	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
672	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
673	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
674	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
675	  nonsingular curve).}
676	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
677	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
678	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
679	  \[
680	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
681	  \]
682	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
683	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
684	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
685	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
686	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
687	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
688	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
689	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
690	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
691	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
692	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
693	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
694	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
695	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
696	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
697	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
698	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
699	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
700	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
701	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
702	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
703	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
704	\end{definition}
705	\begin{theorem}
706	\leanok
707	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
708	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
710	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
711	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
712	  homomorphism
713	  \[
714	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
715	    \qquad
716	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
717	    \qquad
718	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
719	  \]
720	\end{theorem}
721	\begin{proof}
722	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
723	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
724	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
725	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
726	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
727	\end{proof}
728	\section{Principal divisors}
729	\begin{lemma}
730	
731	\leanok
732	[Finite support of the order function — Hartshorne 6.1 / Stacks 02RV]
733	  \label{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_order_finite_support}
735	  % \lean{...} corrected iter-199 review: the iter-199 plan-phase
736	  % pin used the wrong namespace (".Scheme.") and the Lean theorem at
737	  % WeilDivisor.lean:357 sits in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` only
738	  % (Scheme.RationalMap closed at L159). The theorem is also declared
739	  % `private` — sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. The
740	  % chapter pin is retained as documentation for the future-closed
741	  % version; the iter-199 plan agent's directive to consume this via
742	  % the `[IsNoetherian X]` strengthening remains the closure path.
743	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:prime_divisor}
744	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (read from
745	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
746	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
747	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
748	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
749	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (a.k.a.\ Stacks 02RV).}
750	
751	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral scheme that is regular in
752	  codimension one, and let \(f \in K(X)\) be a nonzero rational function.
753	  Then \(\{Y \in X^{(1)} \mid \ord_Y(f) \ne 0\}\) is finite.
754	\end{lemma}
755	
756	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open
757	% affine subset of \(X\) on which \(f\) is regular, \(f \in A\). Then for any
758	% prime divisor \(Y\) which meets \(U\), \(Y \cap U\) corresponds to a minimal
759	% prime ideal \(\mathfrak p\) of \(A\) containing \(f\). But these are finite
760	% in number (II, 3.10). On the other hand, \(X - U\) is a proper closed
761	% subset of \(X\), so it can contain only finitely many prime divisors of
762	% \(X\) [\dots]"
763	\begin{proof}
764	  \uses{def:order_at_point}
765	  Following Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV:
766	  \begin{enumerate}
767	  \item Pick a nonempty affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R \subset X\) on
768	    which \(f\) is regular. Then \(R\) is a Noetherian integral domain.
769	  \item For each prime divisor \(Y\) meeting \(U\), the corresponding
770	    prime \(\mathfrak p_Y\) is a height-1 prime of \(R\); the height-1 primes
771	    containing \(f \cdot 1\) are precisely the minimal primes of
772	    \((f)\), which by Krull's Hauptidealsatz form a finite set (Stacks tag
773	    00KZ / Atiyah--Macdonald 11.16). Of those, \(\ord_Y(f) \ne 0\) holds
774	    only at the finitely many minimal primes of \((f)\) over \(R\).
775	  \item The remaining prime divisors \(Y\) lie in the closed complement
776	    \(X \setminus U\), which is a proper closed subset of \(X\); the
777	    Noetherian topological structure of \(X\) (Stacks 04ZE) bounds the
778	    irreducible components of \(X \setminus U\) of codimension one in
779	    \(X\) by a finite number. For each such component, the stalk
780	    \(\mathcal O_{X, Y}\) is a DVR (regularity in codim 1) and \(f\) has
781	    finite valuation; together these contribute only finitely many
782	    nonzero values to the order function.
783	  \item Combining (2) and (3) gives the finiteness of the support.
784	  \end{enumerate}
785	  \emph{Hypothesis health.} The argument in (3) uses that \(X\) is
786	  Noetherian as a topological space (so \(X \setminus U\) has finitely
787	  many irreducible components). For \([X]\) only locally Noetherian +
788	  integral (without quasi-compactness), one can produce
789	  counter-examples with infinitely many disjoint codim-1 prime
790	  divisors outside \(U\). The Lean signature thus takes
791	  \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]} (or equivalently
792	  \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]}). The hybrid
793	  WeilDivisor file pins the public-facing declaration under the
794	  weaker \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]} hypothesis and exposes the
795	  \texttt{[CompactSpace X]} (or full Noetherian) strengthening
796	  as the substantive sub-claim that this lemma resolves.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{definition}
800	\leanok
801	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point, lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
802	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
803	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
804	  
805	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
806	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
807	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
809	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
810	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
811	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
812	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
813	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
814	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
815	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
816	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
817	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
818	  p.~131.}
819	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
820	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
821	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
822	  \[
823	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
824	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
825	  \]
826	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
827	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
828	  the same definition specialises to
829	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
830	  sum over closed points.
831	\end{definition}
832	\begin{theorem}
833	\leanok
834	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
835	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
836	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
837	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
838	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
839	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
840	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
841	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
842	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
843	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
844	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
845	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
846	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
847	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
848	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
849	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
850	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
851	  \[
852	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
853	    \qquad
854	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
855	    \qquad
856	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
857	  \]
858	\end{theorem}
859	\begin{proof}
860	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
861	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
862	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
863	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
864	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
865	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
866	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
867	  identities.
868	\end{proof}
869	\begin{theorem}
870	\leanok
871	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
872	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
873	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
874	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
875	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
876	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
878	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
879	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
880	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
881	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
882	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
883	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
884	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
885	  \[
886	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
887	  \]
888	\end{theorem}
889	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
890	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
891	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
892	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
893	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
894	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
895	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
896	\begin{proof}
897	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
898	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
899	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
900	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
901	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
902	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
903	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
904	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
905	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
906	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
907	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
908	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
909	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
910	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
911	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
912	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
913	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
914	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
915	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
916	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
917	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
918	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
919	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
920	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
921	\end{proof}
922	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
923	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
924	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
925	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
926	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
927	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
928	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
929	% positive part can have positive degree).
930	\begin{definition}
931	
932	
933	\leanok
934	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
935	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
936	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
937	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
938	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
939	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
940	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
941	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
942	  \[
943	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
944	  \]
945	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
946	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
947	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
948	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
949	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
950	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
951	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
952	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
953	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
954	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
955	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
956	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
957	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
958	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
959	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
960	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
961	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
962	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
963	\end{definition}

1024	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
1025	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
1026	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
1027	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
1028	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
1029	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)

1034	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
1035	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
1036	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
1037	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
1038	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
1039	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
1040	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
1041	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
1042	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
1043	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
1044	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
1045	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
1046	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
1047	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
1048	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
1049	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
1050	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
1051	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
1052	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
1053	  \[
1054	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
1055	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
1056	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
1057	  \]
1058	  where the second equality uses
1059	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
1060	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
1061	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
1062	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
1063	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
1064	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
1065	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
1066	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
1067	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
1068	  \[
1069	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
1070	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1071	  \]
1072	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
1073	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
1074	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
1075	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
1076	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
1077	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
1078	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
1079	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
1080	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
1081	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
1082	  Therefore
1083	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
1084	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
1085	  taking degree completes the proof.
1086	\end{proof}
1087	\section{Divisor class group}
1088	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
1089	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
1090	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
1091	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
1092	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
1093	out of scope here.
1094	\begin{definition}
1095	\leanok
1096	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
1097	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
1098	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
1099	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
1100	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
1101	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
1102	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
1103	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
1104	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
1105	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
1106	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
1107	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
1108	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
1109	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
1110	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
1111	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
1112	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
1113	  \[
1114	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
1115	  \]
1116	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
1117	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
1118	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
1119	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
1120	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
1121	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
1122	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
1123	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
1124	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
1125	\end{definition}
1126	\section{Out of scope}
1127	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
1128	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
1129	\begin{itemize}
1130	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
1131	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
1132	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
1133	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
1134	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
1135	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
1136	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
1137	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
1138	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
1139	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1140	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
1141	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
1142	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
1143	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
1144	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
1145	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
1146	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
1147	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
1148	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
1149	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
1150	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
1151	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
1152	    strictly curve-focused.
1153	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
1154	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
1155	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
1156	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
1157	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
1158	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
1159	\end{itemize}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
150	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
151	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
152	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
153	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
154	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
155	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
156	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
157	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
158	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
159	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
160	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
161	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
162	\end{proof}
