164	\section{Projective dimension}
165	\label{sec:ab_pd}
166	
167	\begin{definition}
168	
169	\leanok
170	  [Projective dimension]
171	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
172	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
173	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
174	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
175	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
176	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
177	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
178	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
179	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
180	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
181	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
182	  \[
183	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
184	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
185	  \]
186	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
187	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
188	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
189	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
190	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
191	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
192	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
193	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
194	\end{definition}
195	
196	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
197	\begin{itemize}
198	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
199	    iff \(M\) is free);
200	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
201	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
202	\end{itemize}
203	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
204	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
205	
206	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
207	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	
211	\leanok
212	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
213	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
214	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
215	  
216	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
217	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
220	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
221	  %  \begin{enumerate}
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
228	  %  \end{enumerate}"
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
230	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
231	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
232	  \(R\)-modules. Then
233	  \begin{enumerate}
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
237	  \end{enumerate}
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
241	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
242	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
243	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
244	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
245	%  from Lemma REF."
246	\begin{proof}
247	  
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
252	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
253	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
254	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
255	  \[
256	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
261	  \]
262	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
263	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
264	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
265	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
269	\label{sec:ab_main}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
275	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
276	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
277	  
278	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
279	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
282	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
283	  %  \(\)
284	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
285	  %  \(\)"
286	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
287	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
288	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
289	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
290	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
291	  \[
292	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
293	  \]
294	\end{theorem}
295	
296	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
297	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
298	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
299	%  \(\)
300	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
301	%  \(\)
302	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
303	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
304	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
305	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
306	%  Proposition REF we see that
307	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
308	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
309	%  \begin{align*}
310	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
311	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
312	%  \ldots,\\
313	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
316	%  \begin{align*}
317	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
319	%  \ldots,\\
320	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
321	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
322	%  \end{align*}
323	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
324	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).

332	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor

336	%  \(\)

341	%  M/xM \to 0

344	%  By Lemma REF we have
345	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
346	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
347	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
348	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
349	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
350	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
351	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
352	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
353	\begin{proof}
354	  
355	  
356	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
357	
358	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
359	  finite free resolution
360	  \[
361	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
362	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
363	  \]
364	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
365	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
366	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
367	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
368	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
369	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
370	  sequences
371	  \[
372	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
373	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
374	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
375	  \]
376	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
377	  yields the chain of inequalities
378	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
379	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
380	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
381	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
382	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
383	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
384	
385	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
386	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
387	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
388	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
389	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
390	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
391	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
392	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
393	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
394	  \[
395	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
396	  \]
397	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
398	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
399	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
400	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
401	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
402	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
403	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
404	  \[
405	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
406	  \]
407	  Substituting yields
408	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
409	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
410	\end{proof}

490	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
491	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
492	  \[
493	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).

496	\begin{proof}
497	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
498	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
499	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
500	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
501	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
502	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
503	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
504	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
505	  \(\mathbb N_\infty\) depth equality goes through
506	  \texttt{ENat.forall\_natCast\_le\_iff\_le}.
507	\end{proof}

509	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
510	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
511	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
512	
513	The inductive step
514	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
515	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula is gated, as of the current pinned
516	Mathlib snapshot, by exactly three absent substrate ingredients
517	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth that is now in hand
518	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
519	decomposition that the proof block of
520	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folds into four bullets:
521	its purpose is to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
522	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps can be formalised
523	independently (and where the dependency graph permits, in parallel).
524	
525	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
526	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
527	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
528	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
529	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
530	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
531	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
532	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
533	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
534	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
535	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
536	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
537	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
538	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
539	
540	\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
541	input to'':
542	\begin{verbatim}
543	                gap (1)
544	       minimal-resolution carving
545	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
546	                  |
547	                  v
548	                gap (3)
549	        snake lemma on minimal
550	        resolution under x in m
551	                  |
552	                  v
553	   +--------------+--------------+
554	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
555	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
556	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
557	                  ^
558	                  |
559	                gap (4)
560	       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
561	       see lem:depth_drops_by_one)
562	\end{verbatim}
563	Gap (4) is already formalised
564	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
565	\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
566	and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
567	assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.
568	
569	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
570	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
571	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
572	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
573	other gaps:
574	\begin{center}
575	  \begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|c|c|}
576	    \hline
577	    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
578	        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\
579	    \hline
580	    (1) & Minimal-resolution carving             & 40--80    & 1--2
581	        & per-syzygy step CLOSED iter-199
582	                                                                & none \\
583	    (2) & Stacks 00MF (``what is exact'')        & 150--200  & 2--3
584	        & absent             & none \\
585	    (3) & Snake lemma on minimal resolution      & ---       & ---
586	        & \textbf{OBVIATED iter-200} & --- \\
587	    (4) & Depth drops by one                     & ---       & ---
588	        & \textbf{CLOSED}    & --- \\
589	    \hline
590	  \end{tabular}
591	\end{center}
592	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
593	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
594	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
595	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
596	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
597	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
598	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
599	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
600	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
601	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
602	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
603	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof.
604	
605	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
606	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
607	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
608	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
609	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
610	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
611	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
612	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
613	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
614	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
615	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
616	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
617	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
618	
619	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
620	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
621	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
622	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
623	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
624	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
625	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
626	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
627	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
628	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
629	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
630	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
631	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
632	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
633	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
634	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
635	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
636	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
637	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
638	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
639	candidate.
640	
641	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
642	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
643	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
644	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
645	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
646	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
647	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
648	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
649	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
650	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
651	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
652	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
653	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
654	helper
655	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
656	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
657	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
658	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
659	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
660	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
661	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
662	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
663	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
664	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
665	inductive code.
666	
667	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
668	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
669	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
670	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
671	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
672	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
673	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
674	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
675	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
676	yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
677	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
678	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
679	dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
680	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget
681	recorded in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}: the refined window is somewhat
682	tighter on the upper end (5--8 vs.\ 6--12 sequential) and is
683	substantially tighter under parallel dispatch (3--5 parallel-best
684	vs.\ 6--12).

686	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
687	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
688	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	
692	\leanok
693	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
694	    over a local ring]
695	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
696	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
697	  \uses{def:depth}
698	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
699	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
700	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
701	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
702	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
703	  %     \ldots
704	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
705	  %     R^{n_i}
706	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
707	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
708	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
709	  %     \ldots
710	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
711	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
712	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
713	  %  complex
714	  %     \ldots \to
715	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
716	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
717	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
718	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
719	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
720	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
723	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
724	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
725	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
726	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
727	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
728	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
729	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
730	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
731	  \begin{enumerate}
732	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
733	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
734	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
735	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
736	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
737	      summands).
738	  \end{enumerate}
739	\end{lemma}
740	\begin{proof}
741	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
742	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
743	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
744	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
745	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
746	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
747	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
748	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
749	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
750	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
751	
752	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
753	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
754	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
755	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
756	
757	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
758	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
759	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
760	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
761	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
762	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
763	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
764	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
765	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
766	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
767	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
768	\end{proof}
769	
770	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
771	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
772	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
773	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
774	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
775	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
776	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
777	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
778	is not needed.

891	
892	\paragraph{Iter-201 Path B closure recipe (LES-injectivity +
893	matrix-collapse).} The iter-201 \texttt{ab-stacks00mf} mathlib-
894	analogist verdict identifies an alternative closure that sidesteps
895	gap (2): instead of building Stacks 00MF as a standalone substrate,
896	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an
897	\emph{inductive step} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)) and a
898	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
899	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new
900	\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC matrix-collapse helper.
901	
902	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
903	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
904	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
905	the contrapositive of
906	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
907	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
908	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
909	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
910	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
911	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
912	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
913	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
914	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
915	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
916	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
917	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
918	contradicting the IH.
919	
920	\textit{Base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) \((\sim 80\)--\(120\) LOC).}
921	\(\ker f\) is projective (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = 0\)), hence free
922	over the local Noetherian \(R\) by Mathlib's
923	\(\texttt{Module.free\_of\_flat\_of\_isLocalRing}\) +
924	\(\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\). So \(\ker f \cong R^m\) with
925	\(m \geq 1\). The inclusion \(\ker f \hookrightarrow R^n\) is an
926	\((n \times m)\)-matrix \(A\) with entries in
927	\(\texttt{Ann}(\kappa) = \mathfrak m\) (from
928	\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}'s minimality
929	clause \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\)).
930	
931	The new \textbf{matrix-collapse helper}
932	(\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC, target site
933	\texttt{AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} L1290 area):
934	\[
935	  \forall i \geq 0,\quad
936	  (- \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,A) \colon
937	  \texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^m) \longrightarrow
938	  \texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^n)
939	  \quad \text{is the zero map}.
940	\]
941	Proof: \(A = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} \cdot E_{ij}\) (basis-matrix sum); by
942	Mathlib's \(\texttt{Ext.add\_comp}\) +
943	\(\texttt{Ext.smul\_comp}\) +
944	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) (from
945	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/Linear.lean}),
946	the postcomp distributes. Each summand has a scalar factor
947	\(A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}(\kappa)\) acting on
948	\(\texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^n)\), killed by the existing
949	\(\texttt{ext\_smul\_eq\_zero\_of\_mem\_annihilator}\) (L229). Sum
950	collapses to zero.
951	
952	With matrix-collapse, the LES at \(i = \texttt{depth}\,R - 1\)
953	gives
954	\[
955	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
956	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}
957	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f)
958	  \xrightarrow{\;(- \circ A)\;}
959	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^n)
960	\]
961	with the second arrow zero. If
962	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,R\), the source is zero,
963	hence \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) = 0\) ---
964	but \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) \simeq
965	\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^m) \neq 0\)
966	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).
967	Contradiction. So \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\), and the
968	base case
969	\(\texttt{pd}\,M + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) follows
970	from \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\) (since \(\ker f\)
971	free).
972	
973	\textbf{Total Path B cost: \(\sim 130\)--\(200\) LOC} comparable to
974	Path A but with better composition --- every new lemma reuses
975	existing axiom-clean primitives, and the matrix-collapse helper is
976	independently useful for any future ``minimal matrix annihilates
977	\(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\)'' argument. Persistent file:
978	\texttt{analogies/ab-stacks00mf.md} (iter-201).

980	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
981	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
982	
983	\begin{definition}
984	
985	\leanok
986	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
987	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
988	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
989	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
990	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
991	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
992	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
993	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
994	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
995	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
996	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
997	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
998	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
999	\end{definition}
1000	
1001	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1002	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1003	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1004	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1005	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1006	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1007	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1008	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1009	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1010	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1011	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1012	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1013	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1014	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
1015	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
1016	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
1017	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
1018	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1019	\begin{corollary}
1020	
1021	\leanok
1022	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1023	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1024	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1025	  
1026	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1027	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1028	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1029	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1030	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1031	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1032	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1033	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1034	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1035	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1036	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1037	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1038	\end{corollary}
1039	
1040	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1041	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1042	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1043	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1044	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1045	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1046	\begin{proof}
1047	  
1048	  
1049	  
1050	  
1051	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1052	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1053	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1054	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1055	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1056	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1057	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1058	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1059	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1060	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1061	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1062	
1063	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1064	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1065	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1066	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1067	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1068	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1069	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1070	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1071	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1072	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1073	\end{proof}
1074	
1075	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1076	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1077	
1078	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1079	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1080	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1081	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1082	
1083	\begin{enumerate}
1084	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1085	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1086	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1087	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1088	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1089	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1090	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1091	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1092	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1093	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1094	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1095	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1096	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1097	\end{enumerate}
1098	
1099	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1100	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1101	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1102	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1103	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1104	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1105

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}
272	
273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
342	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
343	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
344	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
345	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
346	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
347	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
348	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
349	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
350	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
351	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
352	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
353	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
355	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
356	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
357	  % $S'$-modules
358	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
359	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
360	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
361	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
362	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
363	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
364	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
365	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
366	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
367	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
369	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
370	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
371	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
372	  \[
373	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
377	  \]
378	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
379	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
380	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
381	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
382	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
383	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
384	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
385	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
386	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
387	  \[
388	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
389	  \]
390	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
391	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
392	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
397	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
398	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
399	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
400	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
401	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
402	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
403	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
404	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
405	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
406	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
407	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
408	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
409	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
410	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
411	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
412	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
413	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
414	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
415	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
416	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
417	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
418	  dimension equals \(1\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
423	  \begin{enumerate}
424	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
425	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
426	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
427	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
428	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
429	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
430	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
431	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
432	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
434	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
435	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
436	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
437	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
438	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
439	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
440	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
441	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
442	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
443	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
444	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
445	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
447	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
448	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
449	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
450	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
451	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
452	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
453	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
454	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
455	      the conclusion.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	\end{proof}

571	\subsection{Stage 6.A iter-200 substrate: Stacks 00OE 3-step decomposition}
572	\label{subsec:stage6_iib_substrate_iter200}
573	\textit{Source: \texttt{coe-stacks00oe} mathlib-analogist
574	cross-domain-inspiration recipe (iter-200, persistent
575	\texttt{analogies/coe-stacks00oe.md}); 7 axiom-clean
576	\texttt{private} substrate theorems landed in
577	\texttt{CodimOneExtension.lean} L688--L800 under
578	\texttt{namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme}.}
579	
580	The iter-200 Lane COE prover phase decomposed Stage 6.A
581	(\ref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}, Stacks 00OE) into a
582	3-step Mathlib-chain. Steps 1 and 2 landed axiom-clean as
583	project-local \texttt{private} theorems; Step 3 landed in an
584	additive parameterised form awaiting the Jacobian-regular-sequence
585	witness for the relations of the smooth-algebra presentation.
586	
587	\paragraph{Step 1 --- Krull-dim \(\Rightarrow\) height (trivial,
588	\(\sim 1\) line).} Re-export of Mathlib's
589	\(\texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}\) under
590	the project-local name
591	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_localization\_eq\_height\_atPrime}\); reduces
592	the localised Krull-dim goal to a height-of-maximal-ideal goal.
593	
594	\paragraph{Step 2 --- Polynomial-ring maximal-ideal height
595	(\(\sim 60\) LOC).} For \(\texttt{MvPolynomial}\,(\texttt{Fin}\,n)\,k\)
596	with \(k\) a field, every maximal ideal has height \emph{exactly}
597	\(n\). Lower bound:
598	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_ge\_card\_of\_field}\),
599	induction on \texttt{Fin n} via \(\texttt{MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv}\)
600	+ Mathlib's \(\texttt{Polynomial.height\_eq\_height\_add\_one}\) plus
601	the Jacobson contraction
602	\(\texttt{Polynomial.isMaximal\_comap\_C\_of\_isJacobsonRing}\). Upper
603	bound:
604	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_le\_natCard\_of\_field}\)
605	via \(\texttt{Ideal.height\_le\_ringKrullDim\_of\_ne\_top}\) +
606	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.ringKrullDim\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\) +
607	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_eq\_zero\_of\_field}\). Combined: the
608	\texttt{Fin n}-form
609	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_card}\) and the
610	general \texttt{[Finite ι]}-form
611	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_natCard}\) via
612	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.renameEquiv}\) +
613	\(\texttt{RingEquiv.height\_map}\). Capstone packaging the
614	Steps 1+2 composition for any localisation at a maximal ideal:
615	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_localization\_atMaximal\_MvPolynomial}\).
616	
617	\paragraph{Step 3 --- Drop by regular-sequence length (additive form
618	in hand, witness pending).} Step 3 generally:
619	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim}\,(R'_{m'} / (f_j)_{j \in \sigma}) +
620	 |\sigma| = \texttt{ringKrullDim}\,R'_{m'}\). The additive form is
621	landed as
622	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_quotient\_add\_eq\_of\_regular\_sequence}\)
623	via Mathlib's
624	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_add\_length\_eq\_ringKrullDim\_of\_isRegular}\),
625	parameterised over a hypothesis
626	\(\texttt{RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular}\,R'_{m'}\,\texttt{rs}\)
627	that is the iter-201+ residual substrate obligation. Note:
628	\(\texttt{WithBot}\,\mathbb N_\infty\) carries no \texttt{HSub}
629	instance, so the additive form is the natural API; a subtraction
630	form is not available.
631	
632	\paragraph{Iter-201+ Jacobian-regular-sequence witness recipe.} The
633	residual substrate of Stage 6.A is the Jacobian-criterion
634	regular-sequence witness for the relations of an
635	\(\texttt{Algebra.SubmersivePresentation}\).
636	
637	\textbf{API choice} (per iter-201 \texttt{progress-critic route201}
638	flag): the project-local target predicate is Mathlib's
639	\(\texttt{RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular}\), \emph{not}
640	\(\texttt{IsWeaklyRegular}\). Reason: the iter-200 substrate
641	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_quotient\_add\_eq\_of\_regular\_sequence}\)
642	(\texttt{CodimOneExtension.lean} L807) consumes the strong predicate
643	\(\texttt{IsRegular}\) (via Mathlib's
644	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_add\_length\_eq\_ringKrullDim\_of\_isRegular}\));
645	the localised stalk \(R'_{m'}\) is local, so by Mathlib's
646	\(\texttt{IsLocalRing.isRegular\_iff\_isWeaklyRegular\_of\_subset\_maximalIdeal}\)

648	the relations lie in the maximal ideal --- which holds because

806	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',

808	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
809	  % PDF page 147).
810	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
811	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
812	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
813	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
814	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
815	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the

821	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
822	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
823	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
824	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
825	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
826	\end{lemma}

828	\begin{proof}
829	
830	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `` marker on this proof block reflects
831	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
832	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
833	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
834	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
835	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in
836	  % `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` above; that block carries no
837	  % `` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
838	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
839	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper
840	  % `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`

842	  % typed `sorry` on the regularity half pending an iter-185+ prover round.
843	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
844	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
845	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
846	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
847	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
848	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
849	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
850	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
851	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
852	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
853	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
854	
855	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target

869	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
870	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
871	
872	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
873	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
874	point.
875	
876	\begin{theorem}
877	
878	\leanok
879	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
880	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
881	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
882	  
883	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
884	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
885	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
886	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
887	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
888	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
889	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
890	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
891	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
892	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
893	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
894	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
895	
896	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
897	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
898	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
899	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
900	  in \(X\).
901	\end{theorem}
902	
903	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
904	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
905	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
906	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
907	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
908	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
909	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
910	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
911	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
912	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
913	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
914	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
915	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
916	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
917	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
918	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
919	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
920	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
921	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
922	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
923	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
924	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
925	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
926	% which is a contradiction."
927	\begin{proof}
928	  
929	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
930	
931	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
932	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
933	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
934	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
935	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
936	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
937	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
938	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
939	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
940	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
941	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
942	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
943	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
944	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
945	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
946	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
947	
948	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
949	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
950	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
951	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
952	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
953	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
954	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
955	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
956	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
957	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
958	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
959	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
960	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
961	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
962	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
963	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
964	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
965	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
